coredata.dc:title: Eco-friendly composite films for white LEDs based on AIE dyes and ACQ carbon quantum dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guan, Leng
Zhou, Yingwei
Li, Mengli
Shi, Qiang
Yang, Bing
Zhao, Ling
Wang, Qingru
Zhang, Dong
Nie, Zhaogang
coredata.dc:description: Berberine (BBR) is a natural plant dye with aggregation-induced emission (AIE), making it ideal for white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) applications due to its non-toxicity, low-cost, and environmental compatibility. However, BBR-based white LEDs lack a red light component, resulting in high correlated color temperature (CCT) and low color rendering index (CRI). To address this, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be composited with BBR dyes to create high-performance composite materials for white LEDs, as they possess full-spectrum luminescence and are environmentally friendly. However, most CQDs are prone to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which makes it challenging to effectively couple AIE and ACQ chromophores because of their opposing luminescence behaviors. In this study, a facile strategy for coupling AIE-active BBR and ACQ-active CQDs is demonstrated by introducing glycerol into a PVA matrix and utilizing the formed H-bonding network. Results show that the glycerol concentration has a significant effect on the fluorescence of BBR-CQDs composite film. Varying the glycerol concentration can regulate not only the strength of H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA, but also that between BBR and PVA, which results in tunable emission of composite films. Good luminescence can be exhibited from a white LED fabricated by a blue chip and the BBR-CQDs composite film (6.7 v% glycerol). The CIE chromaticity coordinates, CCT and CRI are determined to be (0.35,0.41), 5018 K and 80, respectively. The results show that the BBR-CQDs composite film is a promising and eco-friendly luminescent material for white LEDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Berberine-CQDs composites film
Aggregation-induce emission
Aggregation-caused quenching
H-bonding
White LEDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (the inset: the corresponding HRTEM image), (b) XRD pattern, and (c) Raman spectrum of the CQDs.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) full XPS spectrum, and (c–e) high-resolution C 1s, O 1s, and N 1s XPS spectra of the CQDs.
(a) UV–Vis absorbance, (b) FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths, and (c) FLE spectrum at a 670 nm emission wavelength of the CQDs.

(a) FL spectra of the BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations. (b) FL intensities of the BBR-CQDs composite film at 525 nm and 675 nm as a function of the glycerol concentration.
FL spectra of BBR in water/glycerol mixtures with different glycerol percentages.
FTIR spectra of BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations.
(a–c) The O1s and (d–f) C1s XPS spectra of BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations.
Network structures and formation mechanism of BBR-CQDs composite film.
TG curves of BBR powders and BBR-CQDs composite film (x = 6.7 %).
EL spectra of the white LED devices based on BBR-CQDs composites films with different glycerol concentrations.
Comparison of CCT, CRI and CIE coordinates of the white LED devices based on BBR-CQDs composites with different glycerol concentrations.

head.ce:title: Eco-friendly composite films for white LEDs based on AIE dyes and ACQ carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Shandong 252059, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physical Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Shandong 252059, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. School of Physical Science and Information Technology, Liaocheng University, Shandong 252059, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Information Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Berberine (BBR) is a natural plant dye with aggregation-induced emission (AIE), making it ideal for white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) applications due to its non-toxicity, low-cost, and environmental compatibility. However, BBR-based white LEDs lack a red light component, resulting in high correlated color temperature (CCT) and low color rendering index (CRI). To address this, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) can be composited with BBR dyes to create high-performance composite materials for white LEDs, as they possess full-spectrum luminescence and are environmentally friendly. However, most CQDs are prone to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), which makes it challenging to effectively couple AIE and ACQ chromophores because of their opposing luminescence behaviors. In this study, a facile strategy for coupling AIE-active BBR and ACQ-active CQDs is demonstrated by introducing glycerol into a PVA matrix and utilizing the formed H-bonding network. Results show that the glycerol concentration has a significant effect on the fluorescence of BBR-CQDs composite film. Varying the glycerol concentration can regulate not only the strength of H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA, but also that between BBR and PVA, which results in tunable emission of composite films. Good luminescence can be exhibited from a white LED fabricated by a blue chip and the BBR-CQDs composite film (6.7 v% glycerol). The CIE chromaticity coordinates, CCT and CRI are determined to be (0.35,0.41), 5018 K and 80, respectively. The results show that the BBR-CQDs composite film is a promising and eco-friendly luminescent material for white LEDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a fourth-generation light source, white light-emitting diodes (LEDs) are ubiquitous and form the backbone of modern lighting and display technology. As an important component of white LEDs, fluorescent materials for color conversion are mostly synthesized by chemical methods [1–3]. Although chemical synthesis routes offer the ability to diversely regulate the color rendering performance of white LEDs, they inherently involve complex synthesis process that are expensive and have a detrimental impact on the environment. These factors have limited the large-scale practical applications of these composites. Therefore, natural plant dyes have been receiving considerable interest due to their non-toxic, low-cost and environmentally friendly properties [4–6]. However, most fluorescent dyes suffer from the aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) effect at high concentrations or in the solid state, which restricts their applications to a great extent. To address this issue, much effort has been directed towards the development of alternative fluorescent dyes. In 2001, the concept of aggregation-induced emission (AIE) was put forward by Tang et al. [7], which is in complete contrast to the ACQ effect. Fluorescent chromophores with AIE properties can emit strong fluorescence (FL) in aggregated states, making them ideal for application in white LEDs. To date, some fluorescent dyes with AIE characteristics have been reported [8–10]. Among them, berberine (BBR) is a natural plant dye extracted from Rhizoma Coptidis and Phellodendron Chinese herbs. Due to advancements in large-scale planting and modern extraction, it can be efficiently obtained with the relative ease [10]. In view of the AIE properties of BBR, Xu et al. [11] investigated its FL properties and confirmed that BBR powder is a promising yellow phosphor (the emission peak at 540 nm) for white LEDs with blue light excitation (wavelength of 400–480 nm). To improve the thermal stability of BBR luminescence, Guan et al. [12] synthesized BBR-PVA composite films with cross-linked network structures. The FL intensity of the composite film is found to remain at approximately 74 % of its original value at 393 K. The white LED fabricated using a 450 nm blue chip and the as-synthesized BBR-PVA composite film shows good luminescence performance. However, the device has a high correlated color temperature (CCT >7000 K) and low color rendering index (CRI <80), which is attributed to the lack of a red light component [12]. For this reason, there is needed to explore environmentally friendly materials that can emit red light for stimulated by blue light and make them compound with BBR to prepare high-performance composite fluorescent materials for white LEDs.

A good strategy is to use another AIE natural plant dye to achieve the red light component. However, reported AIE natural plant dyes, including chelerythrine, sanguinarine, quercetin, and myricetin, all emit light in the blue to yellow spectrum [13–15]. To our knowledge, no AIE natural plant dye has been found to emit red light when stimulated by blue light. Therefore, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are chose to complete the red light component due to their fluorescence emission stability, chemical stability and environmental friendliness [16,17]. However, most CQDs are also affected by the detrimental effects of ACQ due to strong π-π interactions in their graphitized cores [18]. Even if CQDs are dispersed into solid matrices such as PVA, their luminous efficiency will be reduced due to aggregation [19]. Moreover, the opposing luminescence behavior makes it difficult to couple AIE and ACQ chromophores to construct high-efficiency composite materials. Some attempts have been made to achieve multicolor emission by integrating AIE and ACQ chromophores into periodic mesoporous organosilicas [20]. However, the fluorophore synthesis strategy requires several steps of reaction and purification, which is time-consuming, costly, and environmentally harmful. Therefore, a synergistic collaboration of AIE and ACQ chromophores through a simple and environmentally method and the fabrication of tunable multicolor emitting materials for practical applications remains an important challenge.

In this report, red emissive CQDs are prepared from mulberry leaves by a simple hydrothermal process, and it is found that the strongest intensity peak is centered at 670 nm under an excitation wavelength range from 390 to 430 nm. To achieve the coupling of BBR and red CQDs in a PVA matrix, glycerol is introduced as a cross-linking agent to form a network via intermolecular H-bonding interactions. Glycerol is an eco-friendly physical cross-linker that can form multiple H-bonds with PVA, which affects the aggregation degree of BBR and CQDs in the PVA matrix. Therefore, this study focuses on the effect of the glycerol concentration on the FL properties of BBR-CQDs composite films. Finally, white LED devices are fabricated using the as-obtained composite films. Good luminescence can be exhibited from a white LED fabricated by a blue chip and the BBR-CQDs composite film (6.7 v% glycerol). The CIE chromaticity coordinates, CCT and CRI are found to be (0.35,0.41), 5018 K and 80, respectively. The results demonstrate that the introduction of glycerol into the PVA matrix and utilization of the formed H-bonding network can produce tunable emission through the synergistic effect of AIE-active BBR and ACQ-active CDs. The BBR-CQDs composite film is a promising and eco-friendly luminescent material for white LEDs.

BBR powders (98 % purity) were produced from Xi'an Huilin Biotech Co., Ltd. (Shanxi, China). PVA, ethanol, and glycerol were purchased from the Guangfu Fine Chemical Research Institute (Tianjin, China). The blue LED chips (430 nm) were acquired from Looking Long Technology Co., Ltd. (Shenzhen, China). Quartz substrates were provided by South China Xiangcheng Technology Co., Ltd. (Guangzhou, China). Deionized water was used throughout the study.

CQDs were synthesized via a facile hydrothermal process. 4 g of fresh mulberry leaves were mixed with 100 mL of ethanol and extracted at room temperature. After 18 h, the ethanolic mulberry leaf extract was obtained by filtering the mixture through Whatmann No. 1 filter paper. 16 mL of the extract solution was mixed with 4 mL of deionized water, and then the mixture was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-equipped stainless steel autoclave (50 mL) and heated at 160 °C for 3 h. The resultant solutions were subsequently centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm filter membrane and further dried by rotary evaporation followed by lyophilization. Finally, CQDs were collected and then dispersed in ethanol for further use.

The BBR-CQDs composite films were deposited on quartz substrates (45 mm × 12 mm × 1 mm) by a dip-coating process. The substrates were ultrasonically washed with ethanol for 20 min and subsequently dried at 60 °C for 1 h. The BBR-CQDs precursor solution was prepared by adding 5 mg of BBR powders, 20 mL of CQDs solution, and a predetermined amount of glycerol (denoted x, x = 0, 1.4, 6.7, 9.1, and 11.4 % in volume) to 50 mL of a 4 % (w/v) aqueous solution of PVA. The pretreated substrates were immersed in the BBR-CQDs precursor solution for 60 s, and then withdrawn at a constant rate. Substrates were subsequently dried in air at 80 °C for 30 min to produce the final BBR-CQDs composite films.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements with CuKα radiation (λ = 0.15406 nm, 30 kV, 20 mA) were performed in order to investigate the structure of as-prepared CQDs. The morphology and size of as-prepared CQDs were characterized using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA). Raman spectra were recorded using the iHR550 grating spectrometer (iHR550, Horiba, Paris, France). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) experiments were carried out with a thermal ESCALAB 250XI multifunctional imaging electron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were obtained using the irtracer-100 FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV–Vis absorption spectra were obtained with a Hitachi U-3010 UV–Vis spectrophotometer. FL and fluorescence excitation (FLE) spectra were measured on an Edinburgh FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer equipped with a 450 W xenon lamp as the excitation source. Thermogravimetric (TG) analysis was performed by means of a TG analyzer (TAQ-600). The electroluminescence (EL) of the white LED devices was obtained on a PR-655 Spectrascan spectrophotometer.

The morphology and structure of as-prepared CQDs were characterized by TEM, XRD and Raman spectroscopy techniques. TEM investigation reveals that the CQDs exist in the form of uniformly distributed nanodots, where the uniform size of the monodisperse sample is about 2.5 nm, as shown in Fig. 1 a. The high-resolution TEM image (inset of Fig. 1a) shows that CQDs have a high degree of crystallinity and have a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) planes of graphite [21]. This indicates that CQDs have undergone a graphitization process. The XRD pattern of as-prepared CQDs powder (Fig. 1b) exhibits an obvious diffraction peak at 23°, which is attributed to the scattering of graphite (002) [22]. The Raman spectrum of the CQDs exhibits two major peaks centered at 1280 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1, corresponding to the disordered D band and the crystalline G band, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1c. This is indicative of the coexistence of disordered carbon and graphitic carbon structures [23]. The intensity ratio of the G band to the D band (IG/ID) is a measure of the crystallinity of the material. A larger intensity ratio IG/ID implies that the degree of graphitization is higher. In the case of the as-prepared CQDs, the IG/ID is found to be 2.64, which proves that the CQDs have good crystallinity and degree of graphitization [24]. The results are consistent with the XRD and TEM analyses.

The surface groups of the CQDs were identified by FTIR and XPS spectra. The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 a) shows obvious vibrational absorption peaks corresponding to O–H and N–H at 3560-3050 cm−1, C–H at 3020-2800 cm−1 and 560-900 cm−1, CO at 1734-1590 cm−1, C–N and CC at 1480-1280 cm−1, and C–O–C at 1046 cm−1 [25–27]. These spectra indicate the presence of various functional groups on CQD surfaces. The full-scan XPS spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 2b) exhibits three typical peaks at 284.1, 399.4, and 532.0 eV, demonstrating that the CQDs are primarily composed of C (75.09 %), N (1.73 %), and O (23.18 %) elements, respectively. This further confirms the graphitization process of the CQDs. In the high-resolution XPS spectra, the C1s spectrum (Fig. 2c) can be deconvoluted into three components: C–C/CC groups (284.3 eV), C–O/C–N groups (285.9 eV), and CO groups (288.0 eV) [28]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.7 and 532.3 eV, corresponding to CO and C–O, respectively [29]. The deconvolution of the N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) reveals three peaks at 399.2 eV (pyridinic-N), 399.9 eV (pyrrolic-N), and 401.7 eV (graphitic-N) [28]. These XPS results are in accordance with the results obtained by FTIR analysis.

The optical properties of the CQDs were also investigated. The UV–Vis spectrum of the CQDs solution (Fig. 3 a) exhibits two absorption peaks at 275 nm and 410 nm, corresponding to π→π* and n→π* transitions, respectively [30]. Under 410 nm excitation, the FL spectrum of the CQDs (Fig. 3b) exhibits a sharp peak at 670 nm with a shoulder at 725 nm, which is consistent with the findings of Xiong et al. [25]. Based on the XRD, TEM, Raman, FTIR, and XPS results, the red emission of CQDs can be assigned to their large conjugated system and high graphitization degree [31]. The FLE spectrum obtained by monitoring the red emission at 670 nm (Fig. 3c) displays multiple peaks, with the optimal excitation wavelength at around 410 nm, which is identical to the absorption spectrum. In addition, it is found that the CQDs exhibit excitation-independent FL behavior in the excitation range of 390–430 nm (Fig. 3b), which can be attributed to the uniform size distribution of the CQDs [32]. Optical properties of CQDs confirm that they can be used to composite with BBR dyes to prepare high-performance composite fluorescent materials for white LEDs.

To effectively combine BBR with the CQDs, BBR-CQDs composite films are synthesized by using PVA as a film-forming agent and glycerol as a cross-linking agent. Considering the excitation efficiency for the BBR and CQDs, 430 nm is selected as the optimum excitation wavelength. The FL spectra of the BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations are shown in Fig. 4 a. Under the excitation of 430 nm, all composite films exhibit two main emission bands centered at 525 nm and 675 nm, which can be attributed to BBR and CQDs, respectively. As the glycerol concentration is increased from 0 to 11.4 %, the FL intensity of BBR gradually decreases. In contrast, the FL intensity of CQDs exhibits an initial increase as the glycerol concentration is increased and achieves a maximum when the glycerol concentration is 6.7 %. Further increasing the concentration of glycerol results in a decrease of the FL intensity, as shown in Fig. 4b. The results confirm that the FL regulation of BBR-CQDs composite films can indeed be achieved by adjusting the glycerol concentration.

The AIE characteristics of BBR were first reported by Tang et al. and the restriction of intramolecular motion (RIM) and the twisted intramolecular charge transfer (TICT) were credited to the occurrence of its AIE phenomenon [10]. To confirm the AIE characteristics of BBR, the viscosity effect was measured by increasing percentages of glycerol to an aqueous solution of BBR (without adding PVA). A continuous enhancement in FL intensity was observed due to the suppression of the molecular vibration in more viscous solutions, as shown in Fig. 5 [33]. However, in this study, with the increase of glycerol concentration the FL intensity of BBR decreased rather than increased, which is due to the presence of PVA in the composite films. Theoretically, one glycerol molecule can form H-bonding with the hydroxyl of three PVA chains. This suggests that glycerol can act as a cross-linking agent to form a network via intermolecular H-bonding interactions [34]. This promotes the dispersion of AIE-active BBR and ACQ-active CQDs in the PVA matrix, thereby altering the FL properties of the composite films.

To investigate the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA, FTIR spectra of BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations were measured, as shown in Fig. 6 . When the glycerol concentration was increased from 0 to 6.7 %, an intense band at 3361 cm−1 corresponding to the stretching vibrations of the O–H functional groups shifted to 3253 cm−1. The shift towards lower wavenumbers indicates an enhancement of the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA [35], which is beneficial for the dispersion of BBR and CQDs in the PVA matrix. Therefore, the FL intensity of AIE-active BBR decreases and the FL intensity of ACQ-active CQDs gradually increases with increasing the glycerol concentration from 0 to 6.7 %, as shown in Fig. 4b.

When the glycerol concentration is further increased to 11.4 %, the position of the band shifts from 3253 to 3287 cm−1, suggesting that the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA have weakened. This trend can be explained by the fact that the addition of excessive glycerol results in the formation of glycerol clusters, which weaken the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA [36]. Weak H-bonding interactions can disrupt the H-bonding network, leading to the reaggregation of CQDs and BBR. The decreases in FL intensity of CQDs can be attributed to the ACQ effect. However, FL intensity of BBR does not increase due to the AIE effect but rather decreases, indicating that there are other factors involved. Additionally, this decrease is accompanied by a red shift in peak position from 530 to 538 nm when the glycerol concentration is increased from 6.7 to 11.4 %, as shown in Fig. 4a. In a previous study by the authors [12], the FL quenching of BBR accompanied by a slight red shift was also observed in BBR-PVA composite films at higher BBR concentrations. Based on experimental and theoretical analyses, the FL quenching behavior was assigned to enhanced O–H⋯O H-bonding interactions between BBR and PVA [37]. Therefore, in this work, it can be speculated that excessive glycerol weakens the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA but enhances the H-bonding interactions between BBR and PVA. To verify this conjecture, the O1s and C1s XPS spectra of BBR-CQDs composite films with different glycerol concentrations (x = 6.7, 9.1 and 11.4 %) were measured. The O1s spectra (Fig. 7 a–c) can be deconvoluted into two peaks, which can be attributed to C–O/C–OH and CO/C–O–C [38,39]. The C1s spectra (Fig. 7d–f) are deconvoluted into three peaks assigned to CO/O–C–O, C–N/C–O/C–OH/C–O–C, and CC/C–H/C–C [40]. The binding energies change with the glycerol concentration, indicating that chemical and bonding environments of oxygen and carbon are altered. In the O1s spectra, the C–O–C binding energy is found to be 531.47, 531.58 and 531.69 eV when the glycerol concentration is 6.7, 9.1 and 11.4 %, respectively. This increase in the binding energies indicates that the electron densities in the oxygen atoms decrease, which arises from the formation of O–H⋯O H-bonding between BBR and PVA that induces the transfer of electrons from the O in the C–O–C groups of BBR to the –OH of PVA [41].

Based on the above analysis, at lower glycerol concentration (x < 6.7 %) the FL intensities of BBR decrease, whereas the FL intensities of CQDs increase as the glycerol concentration is increased. This is ascribed to the enhancement of the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA. At higher glycerol amount (x > 6.7 %), the decrease in FL intensity of CQDs can be explained by the reaggregation of CQDs resulting from the weaker H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA, while the decrease in FL intensity of BBR is due to enhanced H-bonding interactions between BBR and PVA. The network structures of BBR-CQDs composite films and their possible formation mechanism are illustrated in Fig. 8 . Therefore, this work provides a simple method to tune the FL properties of BBR-CQDs composite films via glycerol concentration and its effects on H-bonding interactions.

Thermal stability is an important determinant of the film applicability in white LEDs. In order to determine the thermal stability of the BBR-CQDs composite films, TG analyses of BBR powders and the BBR-CQDs composite film (x = 6.7 %) were carried out, as shown in Fig. 9 . For the BBR-CQDs composite film, a weight loss of 10 % was detected in the temperature range of 303–433 K, which is due to the loss of moisture. Interestingly, a lower weight loss is recorded on the BBR-CQDs composite film as compared to the BBR powders. This is similar to the report of Ou et al. [42], which indicates that the increased thermal stability of the composite films is due to H-bonding interactions. These findings indicate that the BBR-CQDs composite film demonstrates a reasonable thermal stability for white LED applications.

In order to evaluate the potential for practical applications of BBR-CQDs composite films, white LED devices were fabricated by covering them over the commercial 430 nm blue LED chips. The performance of films with different glycerol concentrations (x = 6.7, 9.1 and 11.4 %) was studied. The EL spectra of the devices exhibit one emission band from the blue chip and two emission bands measured for the BBR-CQDs composite films, as shown in Fig. 10 . CCT, CRI and CIE coordinates of white LED devices are listed in Table 1 . All the devices emit warm white light, which is characterized by CCT <7000 K, and CRI >75. Notably, when the glycerol concentration is 6.7 %, the obtained device displays good luminescence, with the CIE chromaticity coordinates, CCT and CRI being (0.35,0.41), 5018 K and 80, respectively. This performance is much better than that of the white LED device based on BBR-PVA composite film [12], confirming the feasibility of the proposed strategy. Thus, it can be concluded that the introduction of glycerol into the PVA matrix and utilization of the formed H-bonding network can produce tunable emission through the synergistic effect of AIE-active BBR and ACQ-active CDs.

In summary, a facile strategy is demonstrated for achieving the effective coupling of AIE-active BBR dyes and ACQ-active CQDs by introducing glycerol into a PVA matrix and utilizing the formed H-bonding network. The FL of the BBR-CQDs composite film can be tuned via glycerol concentration and its effects on H-bonding interactions. At lower glycerol concentration (x < 6.7 %), the FL intensity of BBR decreases with increasing glycerol concentration, while the FL intensity of CQDs increases. This is attributed to the enhancement of the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA, which is further confirmed by FTIR studies. At higher glycerol amount (x > 6.7 %), the FL intensity of CQDs is lower due to the weakening of the H-bonding interactions between glycerol and PVA leading to the reaggregation of the CQDs, whereas the FL intensity of BBR is lower due to an enhancement of the H-bonding interactions between the BBR and PVA. These trends are confirmed by FTIR and XPS investigations. Finally, the BBR-CQDs composite films are combined with 430 nm blue LED chips to prepare white LED devices. The white LED fabricated using the BBR-CQDs composite film (x = 6.7 %) shows CIE chromaticity coordinates of (0.35, 0.41), CCT of 5018 K, and CRI of 80. These results indicate that the BBR-CQDs composite film is a potential eco-friendly film for white LEDs.

Leng Guan: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Data curation. Yingwei Zhou: Investigation, Data curation. Mengli Li: Data curation, Conceptualization. Qiang Shi: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Conceptualization. Bing Yang: Resources, Conceptualization. Ling Zhao: Formal analysis. Qingru Wang: Formal analysis. Dong Zhang: Formal analysis. Zhaogang Nie: Validation, Resources, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. ZR2018MA039and ZR2021MA091), Guangdong Province Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 2021A1515012049), the Key Research and Development Plan Project of Shandong Province (Grant 2023CXPT083), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant 11774071), and the Special Construction Project Fund for Shandong Province Taishan Scholars.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White‐light‐emitting materials constructed from supramolecular approaches

sb:title.sb:maintitle: High-efficiency narrow-band green-emitting manganese(II) halide for multifunctional applications
